sonic_profile.review_summary_en: A classic budget reference. Known for its neutral and balanced reproduction. The official lateral dynamic compliance is 25 µm/mN, making it a high-compliance cartridge suitable for low-mass tonearms. A legendary budget cartridge, the predecessor to the VM95 series. Praised for its energetic and engaging sound with a slightly warm mid-bass bump. Not the most detailed, but offers incredible performance for its price. A legendary broadcast standard. Official dynamic compliance is 5 @ 100Hz; the effective value at 10Hz is widely measured and accepted to be around 10-13. Praised for its musical energy and powerful bass. An evolution of the classic DL-103 with 6N copper coils. Like the standard 103, its official dynamic compliance is 5 @ 100Hz, with an effective 10Hz value around 12. It retains the powerful, dynamic character of the original but offers a more refined, open, and detailed presentation. A discontinued favorite known for its warm, full-bodied, and forgiving sound. Its Dynamic Stabilizer brush aids tracking. The official dynamic compliance of 25 makes it a high-compliance cartridge, though its effective compliance with the brush engaged is lower. A giant-killer in the budget cartridge category. Known for its rich, warm, and musical sound with a powerful bass response that never feels bloated. Praised for being very quiet in the groove. A high-output moving coil (HOMC) cartridge that can be used with a standard MM phono stage. It offers a taste of MC speed and detail, with a clean, agile, and open sound. Some may find it slightly lean in the bass compared to MM cartridges, but it excels in clarity and soundstaging. A high-end MM cartridge from Audio-Technica, known for its refinement and detail. The Shibata stylus provides excellent high-frequency reproduction without being harsh. The sound is smooth, sophisticated, and creates a wide, deep soundstage. A top-tier MM performer. A classic high-compliance cartridge from Ortofon's VMS series lineage. It's known for a very clean, precise, and detailed sound. A neutral performer that requires a low-mass tonearm to shine. A popular and well-regarded LOMC cartridge from the OC9 series. Known for its neutral and highly detailed presentation, speed, and expansive soundstage. The boron cantilever and Line-Contact stylus extract a huge amount of information from the groove. Can lean towards brightness in some systems. A legendary MM cartridge that has been in production for decades. The Gyger S stylus profile provides outstanding detail retrieval and tracking. The sound is known for being big, full-bodied, and dynamic with a strong sense of rhythm and timing (PRaT). It is highly engaging and considered one of the best MM cartridges ever made. A legendary vintage cartridge, famous for its exceptionally flat frequency response and superb trackability. The beryllium cantilever made it very light and stiff. Its sound is famously neutral, smooth, and uncolored, aiming for pure accuracy. The entry point to the famous 'Grado sound'. It's characterized by a lush, rich, and forward midrange that makes vocals and instruments sound very intimate and engaging. It's not the most neutral or detailed cartridge, but it is exceptionally musical and often praised for its organic quality. A highly respected MC cartridge known for its incredible detail and refinement, especially for its price. The MicroLine stylus and boron cantilever provide excellent tracking and a very airy, open top end. It's a very revealing cartridge that excels in complex passages and offers superb stereo imaging. A classic high-output moving coil (HOMC) cartridge that has been a go-to recommendation for decades. It offers a taste of MC speed and detail without requiring a special MC phono stage. The sound is lively, balanced, and detailed, with surprisingly good bass weight. An outstanding and versatile performer. A step-up LOMC from Denon that offers a more refined and nuanced sound than the 103 series. It's known for its excellent tracking ability at a low tracking force and a smooth, balanced, and highly detailed presentation. A great choice for those with medium to low-mass tonearms. A reference-level LOMC from Denon, lauded for its transparency and delicacy. With a very low output and low tracking force, it is capable of retrieving immense detail. The sound is exceptionally refined and creates a holographic, three-dimensional soundstage. Requires a high-quality, quiet phono stage to shine. One of the most popular entry-level cartridges in the world. The 2M Red offers a big, bold, and energetic sound that is immediately engaging. It's a fun listen with powerful bass and a clear top end, though some find the midrange a bit recessed. A great starting point for any new vinyl setup. A popular step-up from the 2M Red. The 2M Blue offers a significantly more open, dynamic, and detailed sound thanks to its nude elliptical stylus. It is clear and precise, but can sound a bit clinical or forward to some. An excellent upgrade that brings more insight into the music without breaking the bank. A major step up in the 2M line, the Bronze uses a Fine Line stylus for excellent tracking and detail retrieval. It trades the forwardness of the Blue for a more balanced, natural, and textured presentation. It is highly detailed but in a more organic way, making it a favorite for those seeking accuracy without sterility. The flagship of Ortofon's 2M series. With its Shibata stylus and boron cantilever, it is designed for maximum information retrieval. The sound is incredibly detailed, neutral, and revealing, sometimes to the point of being analytical. It is exceptionally fast and clean, but can be unforgiving of poor recordings or system mismatches. A step up from the MP-110, the MP-150 offers a more balanced and refined sound. It retains the powerful bass Nagaoka is known for but integrates it better with a smoother midrange and more extended treble. A great all-rounder that combines musicality with more detail. A significant performer in the Nagaoka line, the MP-200 features a boron cantilever for increased stiffness and agility. This results in a much clearer, more detailed, and faster sound compared to its cheaper siblings. It offers a great balance of musical warmth and analytical detail, with excellent imaging. The entry-level model in Sumiko's 'Pearl' series. With a spherical stylus, it's very forgiving of record wear and setup. The sound is warm and musical with a punchy character. A solid, no-fuss performer for budget systems. Rega's top moving magnet cartridge, designed for perfect synergy with their own tonearms thanks to its three-point mounting system. It's known for a dynamic, punchy sound with powerful bass and plenty of detail. It's an exciting and energetic performer. A popular vintage cartridge from the 70s. The Empire 2000 series was well-regarded for its smooth and detailed sound, good channel separation, and wide soundstage. A solid performer that competed with the likes of Shure and Stanton. A legendary professional calibration standard cartridge. Each 681EEE came with its own performance certificate. Its sound is famously neutral, accurate, and honest, designed as a tool for monitoring vinyl cutting. It's not overly warm or euphonic, but praised for its truthfulness. Pickering was the sister company to Stanton. The XV-15 series was known for a punchy, dynamic, and robust sound. Less of a clinical monitor than its Stanton counterpart, the Pickering was often seen as more lively and engaging for home audio use. The included 'Dustamatic' brush was a key feature. Often bundled with Clearaudio's Concept turntable, this cartridge embodies the house sound: clean, clear, and precise. It offers good detail and a neutral balance, providing a solid foundation and a clear upgrade path within the Clearaudio MM family. The Clearaudio Maestro V2 is a high-end moving magnet cartridge renowned for its dynamic and detailed sound signature.  Its boron cantilever contributes to a highly resolving and transparent presentation, while the powerful bass response delivers a compelling and engaging listening experience. The cartridge consistently earns praise for its exceptional performance, frequently exceeding the typical expectations of a moving magnet cartridge. A classic LOMC known for its exceptionally warm, sweet, and musical presentation. It's less about ultimate analytical detail and more about texture, tone, and musical flow. The 'S' class signifies a 0.4mV output. A non-fatiguing cartridge that is a favorite among many for its romantic sound. A legendary high-output MC cartridge, now in its latest revision. The 10x5 is famous for its powerful, dynamic, and exciting sound. It has a fantastic sense of rhythm and drive that makes music sound alive and engaging. It's a benchmark for performance in its price class. A step-up LOMC (Low Output) model from Dynavector. It offers a more refined and detailed sound than the 10x5 while retaining a powerful and controlled bass response. The Micro-Ridge stylus on a solid boron cantilever provides excellent tracking and information retrieval. A very balanced and sophisticated performer. A classic high-output MC that for years has been a popular first step into moving coil sound. It's known for a bold, dynamic, and slightly forward presentation with plenty of punch. It's more resolving than many MMs at its price point and offers a very engaging, if not perfectly neutral, listening experience. This cartridge is frequently praised for its outstanding value. The MicroLine stylus ensures excellent tracking and detailed retrieval, rivaling far more expensive MC cartridges. Its neutral presentation, while highly revealing, may sound bright or analytical depending on system synergy.  Its quiet operation minimizes groove distortion. The successor to the Type III, the V15 Type IV introduced the Dynamic Stabilizer brush. It continued the Shure tradition of neutrality and superb trackability, with some considering it slightly warmer and more forgiving than its predecessor. A landmark design in its own right. The modern successor to the legendary AT95E. The VM95E continues the tradition of offering excellent, balanced performance for a very reasonable price. It's a great all-round performer that is a significant step-up from the most basic conical styli. A step up from the bonded elliptical of the VM95E, the Nude Elliptical stylus on the VM95EN offers lower tip mass, resulting in a more refined and detailed sound. It particularly improves high-frequency performance, making it cleaner and more extended. The entry-level model in the VM95 series with a conical (spherical) stylus. It's robust and very forgiving of worn records. The sound is warm and pleasant, though it lacks the detail and refinement of the more advanced stylus shapes in the series. A great, affordable starting point. The Shibata stylus version in the VM95 line. Originally designed for quadraphonic records, the Shibata shape offers excellent high-frequency detail and a large contact area, reducing record wear. Sonically, it's known for being smooth and rich, with refined highs that are less analytical than the MicroLine version. A legendary cartridge in the world of DJing and turntablism. Famous for its incredibly high output (9.5mV), thunderous bass, and ability to stick to the groove under heavy scratching. It is not an audiophile cartridge; it's a tool designed for powerful, impactful sound and extreme durability. A very popular and versatile MM cartridge from the 70s. It was known for its warm, punchy, and engaging sound that was a great match for the rock and pop music of the era. A solid, reliable performer. The Stanton 500 is one of the most famous and widely used cartridges in history, especially in radio broadcasting and DJing. The 'AL' version is a robust workhorse with a conical stylus, designed for durability and reliable cueing. Its sound is punchy and effective, but not intended for high-fidelity applications. Part of the 680/681 family, the 'SL' version was specifically designed for the demands of slip-cueing in broadcasting and clubs. It offered higher output and a more rugged suspension than its audiophile 'EEE' sibling. The entry point to Ortofon's Quintet MC series. The Red offers a smooth, balanced, and slightly warm sound. It's a very musical and non-fatiguing listen that provides a great introduction to the world of moving coil cartridges without being overly analytical. A step up from the Quintet Red and Blue, the Bronze model features a Nude Fine Line stylus for improved detail retrieval and tracking. The sound is more neutral, clean, and detailed, with better separation and a more transparent window into the recording. A high-end MC cartridge released to celebrate Ortofon's 80th anniversary. It featured a metal body and a Shibata stylus on a boron cantilever. Known for its highly detailed, dynamic, and clean sound, it was a reference-level product that aimed for accuracy and resolution. Part of Ortofon's high-end Cadenza series, the Blue model uses a ruby cantilever and a Gyger 70 stylus profile. It's praised for its open, dynamic sound and its ability to create a vast, three-dimensional soundstage. It offers a great balance between detail retrieval and musical engagement. A high-output MC cartridge in Sumiko's Reference series. The use of a boron cantilever provides a clear, fast, and transparent sound with excellent detail. It offers a more neutral and revealing presentation compared to Sumiko's warmer, classic offerings. A legendary DJ cartridge, part of the Concorde series. Designed for the high demands of nightclub environments, it offers high output, a punchy sound, and excellent durability. While great for DJing, it is not primarily intended for critical home listening. A famous high-compliance cartridge from the 70s. The ADC XLM (eXtra Low Mass) was renowned for its incredibly low tracking force and delicate, airy sound. It offers superb detail and a sense of effortlessness, but requires a very low-mass tonearm to function correctly. Its bass presentation is light and agile rather than powerful. A legendary high-end MM cartridge from German manufacturer Elac. Featuring a van den Hul stylus on a boron cantilever, it was a technical tour de force designed for maximum resolution. The sound is extremely detailed and neutral, sometimes considered analytical, but its ability to resolve information was world-class. A top model in Grado's entry-level Prestige series. The Gold model is selected for tighter tolerances and offers a more refined version of the classic Grado sound: a warm, rich, and highly textured midrange that is captivating on vocals and acoustic instruments. A very popular and well-regarded cartridge from Ortofon's past. The VMS (Variable Magnetic Shunt) series was known for excellent tracking and a clean, clear, and well-balanced sound. A high-compliance design that works best in low-mass tonearms. The final and most advanced iteration of the legendary Shure V15 series. With an extremely thin beryllium cantilever and a Micro-Ridge stylus, it was a benchmark for trackability and neutrality. It was designed to be as accurate a transducer as possible, and is still considered a reference cartridge by many. The Hana SL (Low Output) gained immense popularity for offering a level of musicality and refinement typically found in much more expensive cartridges. It's known for its wonderfully textured and balanced 'analog' sound, with a Shibata stylus providing excellent detail without any hint of harshness. A modern classic. A solid MM cartridge often bundled with Dual turntables in the 70s. It provided reliable, well-balanced performance that was characteristic of the era's Hi-Fi sound. A competent and musical performer. A precursor to the OM series, the F 15 was a popular and well-regarded high-compliance cartridge from Ortofon. It offered a clean, clear, and neutral sound, designed for the low-mass tonearms prevalent in the late 70s and early 80s. A classic cartridge from Philips, known for its smooth and musical sound. Often found on Philips' own turntables, it was a high-quality performer for its time, with a characteristically pleasant and warm presentation. A high-quality MM cartridge from Sony's golden era. The use of a carbon fiber cantilever was an advanced feature for its time, contributing to a clean, detailed, and precise sound. A well-engineered and excellent sounding vintage cartridge. The top-of-the-line moving magnet cartridge from Austrian manufacturer AKG. It was a high-compliance design known for its very neutral, airy, and detailed sound, with superb stereo imaging. A true high-fidelity performer from the vintage era. Excel Sound was a major Japanese OEM manufacturer that also sold cartridges under its own name. The ES-70 was a well-regarded series, offering balanced, clear, and reliable performance that was competitive with major brands. Clearaudio's flagship Moving Magnet cartridge. The Charisma V2 is a statement product, delivering a huge, authoritative sound with powerful dynamics and an expansive soundstage. It aims to provide MC-level performance with the convenience and output of an MM design. The entry point to Goldring's E-series. The E1 uses a carbon-reinforced cantilever and a spherical stylus to provide a warm, musical, and forgiving sound. A solid upgrade over the most basic, unbranded cartridges. The middle child in the entry-level E-series, the E2 upgrades the cantilever to aluminum from the E1's carbon. This results in a slightly tighter, more precise sound with better definition. The sweet spot in Goldring's entry-level E-series. It offers a significant step up by moving to an elliptical stylus, resulting in a more balanced, smoother, and texturally rich presentation. It's known for its musical coherence and represents excellent value. The flagship of the E-Series, the E4 features a nude elliptical stylus on a hollow aluminum cantilever. This significantly reduces tip mass, resulting in a more detailed, open, and refined sound, especially in the high frequencies. It brings a level of sophistication not usually found at this price point. The entry point to the classic 1000-series. The 1006 offers a dynamic, warm, and energetic sound that is a hallmark of the series. A powerful performer with a standard elliptical stylus. A step-up in the 1000-series featuring a Gyger II line-contact stylus. This provides significantly improved detail retrieval and tracking compared to the 1006, resulting in a clearer and more refined sound while retaining the series' dynamic punch. A higher-end model in the 1000-series using the 'Vital' stylus profile. It offers a very refined and smooth presentation with excellent detail, considered a step between the Gyger II and the top-of-the-line Gyger S of the 1042. The entry point to Goldring's 2000-series of moving iron cartridges. The 2100 with its elliptical stylus offers a warm, smooth, and musical sound. A small step up from the 2100, the 2200 offers a slightly more refined version of the same warm and musical moving iron sound. Upgrading to a 'Vital' line-contact stylus, the 2300 provides a significant jump in detail and openness compared to the elliptical models in the 2000-series. A very smooth and sophisticated sound. The 2400 builds on the 2300 with an improved generator, offering a more dynamic presentation and better channel separation, leading to a wider soundstage. The flagship of the 2000-series. The 2500 uses a high-end '2SD' line-contact stylus on a tapered aluminum cantilever, delivering a fast, agile, and highly detailed sound that represents the pinnacle of Goldring's moving iron technology. A long-standing model in Goldring's MC lineup. The Elite is a low-output MC known for its neutral, detailed, and dynamic performance. It's a classic example of a high-quality moving coil cartridge with a clean, precise sound. The Legacy is a low-output MC designed for a rich, weighty, and powerful sound. It has a more full-bodied and less analytical character than the Elite, focusing on musicality and tonal richness. Goldring's flagship moving coil cartridge. The Ethos is designed for ultimate transparency and detail. It's known for creating a vast, holographic soundstage and retrieving incredible amounts of information from the groove in a neutral and uncolored way. A true high-end reference cartridge. A benchmark in its price class, the Lyra Delos is renowned for its neutrality, transparency, and dynamic expression. It uses a pre-aged damping system to maintain performance over time. It's a highly detailed and revealing cartridge without being sterile, offering a clear window into the recording. A legendary MC cartridge famous for the 'Koetsu sound'. The Rosewood model offers a lush, harmonically rich, and tonally dense presentation with a magical midrange that makes vocals and instruments sound incredibly lifelike. It prioritizes musical beauty and emotional engagement over ultimate analytical detail. The high-output version of the acclaimed Hana SL. The 'SH' offers the same refined and musical presentation with a Shibata stylus, but its 2.0mV output allows it to be used with standard MM phono stages. It's known for its balance, excellent texture, and a slightly sweet, engaging sound. A direct descendant of the original 1959 SPU (Stereo Pick Up). This classic model offers a weighty, dynamic, and tonally rich sound that is beloved for its presentation of classical music and jazz. It's not about hyper-detail, but about scale, authority, and musical substance. A high-end MM cartridge from A-T's 700 series. The VM740ML offers exceptional detail retrieval and channel separation thanks to its MicroLine stylus and dual magnet design. The sound is very clean, precise, and neutral, though it can lean towards brightness in some systems. A top-tier MM tracker. Part of Grado's Timbre series, the Sonata3 features a cured Jarrah wood body. It delivers a more refined and dynamic version of the famous Grado sound. It is characterized by an exceptionally rich, vivid, and textured midrange, making it a favorite for vocals and acoustic music. The Soundsmith Otello is a highly regarded moving iron cartridge renowned for its smooth yet detailed sonic character.  It delivers excellent stereo imaging and impressively low surface noise, creating a refined listening experience.  A popular choice for audiophiles seeking a sophisticated entry point into the Soundsmith product line. Rega's entry-level MC, the Ania delivers the company's signature pace, rhythm, and timing (PRaT). It's a fast, energetic, and engaging listen with plenty of detail. Designed for perfect synergy with Rega's own tonearms and phono stages. A legendary tool for turntablists. The Concorde Scratch is engineered for one primary purpose: to stay in the groove under the most demanding scratching and back-cueing. It has an extremely high output for a powerful, punchy sound and is built for maximum durability. A very popular and common 'workhorse' cartridge from ADC in the late 70s. The QLM (Qualified Low Mass) series was a step below the flagship XLM, offering a more moderate compliance suitable for a wider range of tonearms. It provides a balanced, smooth, and pleasant sound, making it a great all-round performer. A legendary Japanese MC cartridge, the Shelter 501 III is famous for its exceptionally musical and engaging sound. It combines warmth and richness with sufficient detail to be captivating but never fatiguing. It has a powerful and authoritative bass response and a smooth, liquid midrange. A favorite for those who prioritize musical enjoyment. A high-end MC from Clearaudio featuring a 24-karat gold coil and an ebony wood body. It embodies the Clearaudio house sound of precision, detail, and neutrality. It is highly dynamic and clean, with superb tracking and a very wide frequency response, revealing fine nuances in the recording. The audiophile sibling to the 681EEE, the Stanton 881S was a top-tier MI cartridge competing with the Shure V15. It featured a nude Stereohedron line-contact stylus for exceptional tracking and detail retrieval. Its sound is neutral, clean, and highly refined, with outstanding channel separation. A modern P-Mount (T4P) standard cartridge from Audio-Technica. It provides an excellent and affordable way to keep vintage linear tracking and other P-Mount turntables running. The sound is clear, balanced, and offers good detail for its class, representing a solid upgrade over older, worn styli. A classic MC cartridge from A.J. van den Hul. The DDT (Depth, Detail, Timbre) II Special is famous for its natural and non-fatiguing sound, coupled with high levels of detail and transparency. It uses a VDH-I stylus on a boron cantilever to achieve a presentation that is both highly accurate and musically satisfying. Sumiko's long-standing entry-level cartridge. The Oyster provides a warm, pleasant, and musical sound that is very forgiving of less-than-perfect records. It is often a stock cartridge on turntables like the Pro-Ject Debut series and offers a great, no-fuss introduction to vinyl. A legendary top-of-the-line cartridge from Empire, designed for CD-4 quadraphonic playback. It featured a '4-Dimensional' nude stylus (a Shibata variant) on a vapor-deposited beryllium cantilever, a technical marvel for its time. It is known for its incredible high-frequency extension, detail, and airy presentation. An entry point into the world of ZYX cartridges, a high-end Japanese manufacturer. The Bloom 3 is known for its 'Real Stereo' generator system which aims for perfect time and phase alignment. The result is a fast, clean, and transparent sound with excellent rhythm and pace, offering a taste of true high-end performance. An immensely popular cartridge from the 70s, the M91ED was a workhorse that offered a large portion of the performance of the top-tier V15 at a much lower price. It's known for its warm, full, and musical presentation with a solid bass foundation. A classic high-compliance design that was a great match for the tonearms of its day. Part of Audio-Technica's 'XP' line, the AT-XP7 is designed as a hybrid cartridge for both DJ use and high-quality listening. It features a robust suspension and high output for the demands of a DJ booth, but its elliptical stylus provides better fidelity and detail than a typical conical DJ stylus. A great choice for a versatile setup. The flagship of Hana's M-Series, the ML (Microline, Low-output) offers a significant step up in resolution and transparency. The nude Microline stylus on a boron cantilever extracts immense detail, presenting it in a neutral and coherent fashion. It's praised for its superb stereo imaging and quiet backgrounds, competing with far more expensive cartridges. Designed by Mobile Fidelity Sound Lab, the UltraTracker aims to be a neutral transducer, faithful to the original master tape. It uses a V-Twin dual-magnet generator and a nude elliptical stylus to achieve a well-balanced and dynamic sound with excellent clarity and trackability. It's praised for its honest and engaging presentation. The flagship of the Cadenza series, the Black is a reference-level cartridge designed for ultimate information retrieval. It features a Nude Shibata stylus on a boron cantilever and uses silver-plated copper wire. Its sound is exceptionally detailed, neutral, and transparent, creating a vast and precisely rendered soundstage. It can be unforgiving of poor recordings but is stunning on good ones. A legendary MC cartridge famous for its unique 1.7mm solid diamond cantilever, which is exceptionally short and rigid. This design results in an incredibly fast, dynamic, and detailed sound with sharp transients and precise imaging. The Karat is praised for its clarity and excitement, offering a vivid and immediate presentation. Pickering's top-of-the-line moving iron cartridge and a direct competitor to the Shure V15 and Stanton 881S. It featured a nude Stereohedron line-contact stylus for superb tracking and low wear. Its sound is often described as slightly warmer and smoother than the Stanton, offering a highly refined, detailed, and musical experience. Famous for its 'Dustamatic' brush. The top model in B&O's MMC 20 series, the 'CL' (Contact Line) was a technical marvel. It featured a single-crystal sapphire cantilever and a nude Contact Line diamond, resulting in extremely low effective tip mass. The sound is highly accurate, detailed, and refined, designed for the very-low-mass tonearms on B&O's linear tracking turntables. The Linn Krystal is a moving coil cartridge designed to work in harmony with Linn tonearms, particularly the Ekos SE. It follows the Linn philosophy of 'source-first' and prioritizes musicality, rhythm, and timing (PRaT). It's a dynamic and engaging listen that gets your foot tapping, while the Vital stylus pulls out plenty of detail. A high-performance MC from Rega, the Apheta 3 builds on the Ania with a more advanced generator and a fine line diamond profile. It is incredibly fast, transparent, and detailed, revealing layers of information in a highly dynamic and coherent way. It represents a further refinement of Rega's 'zero-tolerance' engineering philosophy. The Grado Prestige Green is a specially selected version of the Black model, chosen from the production run for meeting higher test specifications (typically better channel balance). It offers the classic warm, rich Grado sound with its famous forward midrange, making it highly musical and engaging, especially with vocals. Part of Audio-Technica's flagship ART (Audio-Technica Reference Transducer) series, the AT-ART9XA is a non-magnetic core MC cartridge. This design is known for producing a sound with exceptional transparency, texture, and natural warmth. It is less analytical than its magnetic-core siblings, prioritizing a rich, refined, and deeply musical presentation. A legendary high-output MC known for its open-body design. The 'Evo III' offers a highly detailed, dynamic, and open sound with an expansive soundstage. The boron cantilever contributes to its speed and clarity. It's a long-standing recommendation for those wanting a taste of high-end moving coil sound without needing a dedicated MC phono stage. A legendary vintage MM cartridge from Technics' golden era. It featured a pure boron pipe cantilever, a feat of engineering at the time, resulting in incredibly low tip mass. The sound is famously neutral, transparent, and detailed, designed to be a high-precision transducer. It requires a low-mass tonearm to perform at its best. The quintessential professional broadcast cartridge. The EMT TSD 15, often in its integrated 'Tondose' shell, is famous for its authoritative, dynamic, and deeply musical presentation. It's not about the last word in airy detail, but about substance, powerful dynamics, and tonal realism. A legend in the audio world. A highly regarded and distinctive cartridge design. The absence of a conventional cantilever, coupled with its direct-scanning mechanism, contributes to its remarkable speed, dynamic range, and vivid sound reproduction.  Known for its realistic portrayal of music, it demands careful setup. A top-tier MM from A-T's 700 series, featuring a Shibata stylus. Compared to its MicroLine sibling (VM740ML), the VM750SH is often described as smoother, richer, and more forgiving. It provides excellent detail and refined high frequencies without any hint of stridency, making it a highly musical and sophisticated performer. Another classic vintage Shure cartridge, positioned just below the famous V15 series. The M95ED was praised for its warm, punchy, and highly musical performance. It was an excellent tracker and a fantastic all-rounder for the music of its time, capturing the energy of rock and pop perfectly. A high-compliance design for low-mass arms. The Benz-Micro ACE (Advanced Cartridge Engineering) has been a benchmark for mid-priced MCs for years. The 'S' model (low output) is known for its supremely balanced and musical nature. It combines detail and refinement with a touch of warmth, making it non-fatiguing and emotionally engaging. A true classic. A true mono cartridge designed for playing mono microgroove records. Instead of strapping stereo channels together, it is internally wired to only respond to lateral movement, which dramatically reduces noise from wear and dirt on mono LPs. It provides a stable, powerful, and focused sound that is a revelation for any serious mono collector. Positioned above the Delos, the Lyra Kleos offers even greater resolution, dynamic contrast, and transparency. It builds upon the Lyra house sound of neutrality and detail, creating a holographic and precisely imaged soundstage. It's an exceptionally revealing and engaging cartridge that represents a clear step into high-end audio. The flagship of Nagaoka's Moving Permalloy (MI) line. The MP-500 is a world-class cartridge that combines the best attributes of MM and MC designs. It's incredibly detailed and balanced, yet retains a smooth, rich musicality that Nagaoka is famous for. Its tracking is superb, and it is widely regarded as one of the very best fixed-coil cartridges available. A revival of the legendary Kiseki brand from the 1980s, the Blue N.S. (New Style) captures the magic of the original. It's known for a richly hued, colourful, and highly musical presentation. It's less about sterile neutrality and more about conveying the emotion and texture of the music, with impressive dynamics and a very engaging character. A mid-tier offering from Peter Ledermann's Soundsmith, the Carmen Mk II is a high-output MI cartridge praised for its sweet and musical nature. It offers excellent detail retrieval and is particularly noted for creating a holographic, three-dimensional soundstage. A very sophisticated performer for its price. Rega's long-standing mid-tier MM cartridge. The Elys 2 embodies the company's focus on pace, rhythm, and timing (PRaT). It's an energetic, punchy, and fun listen that excels at conveying the drive of the music. It uses Rega's three-point mounting for easy setup on Rega tonearms. A legendary and now-discontinued flagship MC from Denon. The DL-S1 was a statement piece, designed for ultimate resolution and refinement. Using a solid boron cantilever and a very low-output generator, it delivered a sound of incredible delicacy, transparency, and detail, far removed from the powerful character of the DL-103. Requires a top-tier system to shine. A classic MM cartridge from Linn in the 80s and 90s, often bundled with their turntables. The K9 (based on an Audio-Technica generator) was designed to deliver the characteristic Linn 'PRaT' (Pace, Rhythm, and Timing). It's a fun, punchy, and highly engaging cartridge that excels at making music sound exciting. The flagship of the standard OC9X series, the XSL (Special Line contact) is designed for maximum information retrieval. It uses A-T's most advanced stylus profile on a boron cantilever. The sound is incredibly transparent, fast, and detailed, with superb tracking. It can be considered analytical but is unmatched in its ability to resolve fine detail at its price point. A legendary vintage MM cartridge, often paired with Grace's own highly regarded tonearms. The F-9 series was famous for its smooth, refined, and effortlessly musical presentation. The 'E' model with its beryllium cantilever offered superb detail retrieval without ever sounding harsh. A true classic of the golden age of hi-fi. An iconic moving coil cartridge from the 1970s. The Supex SD-900 is historically significant as its designer, Yoshiaki Sugano, later founded Koetsu. It established the template for the 'classic' Japanese MC sound: warm, rich, and intensely musical, with a focus on tonal beauty over analytical detail. The ancestor of many famous cartridges. The flagship MM cartridge from Mobile Fidelity. The MasterTracker upgrades from the UltraTracker with a Micro-Line stylus, providing enhanced detail retrieval, improved high-frequency extension, and superior tracking. It maintains the house sound of neutrality and dynamism, aiming to get the listener as close as possible to the sound of the master tape. An upgraded version of the Quintet Black, the 'S' model swaps the boron cantilever for a sapphire one. This results in an even faster, cleaner, and more refined presentation. The Shibata stylus offers superb detail retrieval, and the sapphire cantilever adds a level of rigidity and speed that enhances transparency and transient response. A classic and hugely popular MM cartridge from the 1960s and 70s. The G800 was a robust and reliable performer known for its warm, powerful, and weighty sound. It was very forgiving and a great match for the pop and rock music of the era. A true vintage workhorse. A high-end MC from ZYX that lives up to its name. The Ultimate Airy is renowned for its open, transparent, and incredibly fast presentation. It uses ZYX's 'Real Stereo' generator to create a stunningly realistic and holographic soundstage. It is a benchmark for speed and transparency, offering a 'you are there' listening experience. A limited-edition MC released to celebrate Sumiko's 40th anniversary. It builds on the long-running Pearwood Celebration II with an upgraded stylus and generator. The sound is highly refined, spacious, and musical, with a rich textural quality and excellent balance. It combines detail with a natural warmth and flow. A legendary workhorse cartridge for broadcasting and general DJ use. The SC35C is known for its extreme durability and ability to track under difficult conditions. Its sound is warm and punchy, prioritizing reliability and a solid fundamental tone over high-frequency detail. A great choice for playing worn records or for heavy-duty applications. Arguably the most common entry-level cartridge in the world, the AT3600L is found on countless budget turntables (often unbranded or rebranded, like the Rega Carbon). For its extremely low cost, it provides a surprisingly warm, musical, and enjoyable sound. It's robust, forgiving, and gets the basics of musical enjoyment right. Sitting between the MP-200 and MP-500, the Nagaoka MP-300 is a high-performance MI cartridge. It combines a nude elliptical stylus with a boron cantilever to deliver a sound that is clear, detailed, and dynamic, with excellent balance across the frequency range. It's a significant step up in refinement and resolution from the lower models. A long-standing favorite in the upper-mid tier of MC cartridges. The Dynavector XX-2 MkII is known for its powerful and dynamic presentation, underpinned by a solid and articulate bass foundation. It combines this energy with a high degree of refinement and detail, making it a superb all-rounder that can handle any musical genre with aplomb. The entry point to the legendary Ortofon SPU family, the #1 S (Spherical) offers the classic, weighty, and rich SPU sound at a more accessible price. It's a perfect introduction to this iconic line of cartridges, delivering a powerful and musical performance, especially on classic jazz and orchestral recordings. Requires a high-mass tonearm. A unique low-output Moving Iron cartridge from Soundsmith. The Zephyr MIMC (Moving Iron for MC Preamp) Star offers performance that competes with high-end MCs. It is known for its incredible dynamics, speed, and detail retrieval, thanks to its Boron cantilever and Contact Line stylus. It provides a highly resolved and exciting listening experience. A modern high-end MC from EMT, the JSD 6 combines the company's legendary authoritative and dynamic sound with modern refinement. The Gyger S stylus on a boron cantilever provides excellent detail retrieval and tracking. It's a powerful yet sophisticated cartridge that brings studio-level realism and musicality to a home system. A legendary MC cartridge from Linn in the 1980s, succeeding the Asak. The Karma was famous for its incredible sense of pace, rhythm, and timing (PRaT), making music sound incredibly alive and engaging. It had a punchy, dynamic character that was a perfect match for the Linn LP12/Ittok combination. The AT-VM540ML is a highly regarded MM cartridge known for its exceptional value and performance.  Its MicroLine stylus ensures superior tracking and detailed retrieval across a wide frequency range. The sonic signature is characterized by neutrality and clarity, making it a versatile choice for various musical genres.  Its ability to resolve fine details without sounding analytical positions this cartridge as a standout in its class. A highly acclaimed MC cartridge from Japanese artisan Miyajima. The Shilabe uses a unique cross-ring generator and a bamboo cantilever to produce an incredibly powerful, weighty, and tonally dense sound. It is renowned for its emotionally direct and visceral presentation, focusing on the body and soul of the music over analytical minutiae. European Audio Team's entry-level moving coil cartridge. The Jo N°5 is notable for its mint green nylon body, produced via selective laser sintering. It offers a dynamic, smooth, and refined sound with a wide soundstage, making it a very capable and stylish performer in its price class. Manufactured by Ortofon to EAT's specifications. The entry-level cartridge from high-end Japanese manufacturer Phasemation. The PP-200 inherits technology from its more expensive siblings, offering a natural, spacious, and detailed sound. It is praised for its ability to convey the emotional content of music, combining high-fidelity resolution with a deeply musical character. A high-end version of the SPU designed for standard headshells ('N' for naked, no integrated shell). The SPU Royal N uses Ortofon's top-tier Replicant 100 stylus and silver coils to blend the classic SPU power and richness with a much higher degree of detail, refinement, and high-frequency extension. A truly reference-level SPU. A Concorde-style cartridge made for Pro-Ject by Ortofon. The Pick-IT S2 C is designed for both DJs and hi-fi enthusiasts, offering a robust build, high output, and an SME-style connection for easy mounting. Its sound is lively and punchy, providing a fun and energetic presentation. A top-tier, low-output Moving Iron cartridge from Soundsmith. The Paua features a single-crystal ruby cantilever and an advanced line-contact stylus. It is known for its world-class resolution, speed, and dynamic range, creating a holographic and deeply immersive soundstage. It offers all the benefits of high-end MCs with the user-replaceable stylus advantage of MI designs. A classic and highly-regarded MM cartridge from the legendary Garrott Brothers of Australia. The P77 is known for its incredibly musical, liquid, and smooth presentation. The Micro-Tracer line-contact stylus ensures excellent detail retrieval, but the overall character is one of effortless musical flow, making it a favorite among many audiophiles. The successor to the famous XLM, the ZLM (Z-LOMASS) was ADC's top-tier induced magnet cartridge. It featured a nude 'Astatic' line-contact stylus for even better tracking and high-frequency resolution. Like its predecessor, it's an extremely high-compliance design known for a delicate, detailed, and airy sound. Requires a very low-mass tonearm like the Infinity Black Widow. Introduced in 1958, the Shure M3D holds a significant place in audio history as one of the first commercially successful stereo cartridges. Its low compliance and high tracking force were characteristic of the era and well-suited to the records and tonearms then available. This cartridge is known for its warm, powerful, and punchy sound, delivering an energetic and engaging presentation of early stereo recordings. A limited-edition cartridge created to celebrate Ortofon's 95th anniversary. The MC A95 uses a unique body made via Selective Laser Melting (SLM) and features the company's best technologies, including the Replicant 100 stylus. It's a true reference transducer, known for its incredible speed, transparency, and neutrality, revealing every nuance of the recording with breathtaking clarity. A statement-level cartridge from Grado, part of their Lineage series. The Statement3 uses a specially-cured Jarrah wood body and a low-output generator. It represents the pinnacle of the 'Grado sound': an incredibly rich, textured, and lifelike midrange, combined with a huge, enveloping soundstage. It is a deeply emotional and immersive listening experience. The true mono sibling to the famous DL-103. The DL-102 is a high-output moving coil cartridge specifically designed for playing mono records. It is internally wired for mono and produces a powerful, solid, and punchy sound that brings vintage mono recordings to life with authority and drive. A broadcast standard for decades. A special edition MM cartridge celebrating Clearaudio's 40th anniversary. The Jubilee MM uses a body made from Panzerholz (bulletproof wood) for its excellent resonance-damping properties. With a Gyger S stylus on a boron cantilever, it delivers a highly detailed, clean, and dynamic sound that is characteristic of Clearaudio's high-end MM designs. The entry point to the Hana line, the EH (Elliptical, High-output) has been widely praised for offering exceptional musicality for its price. It provides a balanced, smooth, and engaging sound that makes it a fantastic first step into the world of moving coil cartridges, without requiring a special phono stage. Part of A-T's flagship ART series, the ART7 is a non-magnetic core MC. This design eliminates magnetic distortion, resulting in a sound that is exceptionally warm, natural, and textured. It is less about analytical precision and more about capturing the organic flow and emotional content of the music. Requires a very high-quality, quiet phono stage due to its extremely low output. Linn's first moving coil cartridge, introduced in the late 1970s. The Asak (built by Supex) was a powerful and dynamic performer that perfectly complemented the Linn Sondek LP12. It had a raw, bold energy that was thrilling and engaging, helping to establish the 'Linn sound' and the philosophy of Pace, Rhythm, and Timing (PRaT). The entry-level moving coil from German turntable manufacturer Acoustic Signature. The MCX1 is a solid, well-built cartridge that offers a smooth, balanced, and musical performance. It provides a great introduction to the brand's sound, focusing on musical coherence and a pleasant, non-fatiguing listen. A limited-edition, high-fidelity version of the iconic Concorde cartridge, released for Ortofon's 100th anniversary. The Century combines the convenience of the Concorde design with true audiophile components, including a Nude Fine Line stylus and silver-plated copper wire. The sound is highly detailed, balanced, and refined, offering a significant upgrade over standard DJ Concordes. A very common and well-regarded P-Mount (T4P) cartridge from Technics, often found on their linear tracking turntables of the 1980s. The EPC-P202C provided clear, balanced, and reliable performance. It was a solid workhorse that tracked well and delivered a pleasing hi-fi sound. The entry point to A-T's modern OC9X moving coil series. The XEB (Elliptical Bonded) provides an accessible taste of the OC9 sound. It is musical, energetic, and smooth, offering a clear step up in speed and refinement from most MM cartridges without being overly analytical. An excellent value proposition. A precursor to the legendary 10x5, the Dynavector 10x4 MkII was a highly-regarded high-output MC in its own right. It established the Dynavector reputation for creating powerful, dynamic, and rhythmically exciting cartridges that could be used with a standard MM phono stage. A fun and engaging classic. A close sibling to the historic M3D, the Shure M7D was another of the company's pioneering stereo cartridges from the late 1950s. It shares the same low-compliance, heavy-tracking characteristics and delivers a similarly warm, powerful, and punchy sound. A fantastic choice for bringing life to early stereo LPs on vintage high-mass tonearms. The long-standing entry point to the world of Koetsu. The Black Goldline delivers the legendary 'Koetsu sound': a presentation that is incredibly lush, harmonically rich, and tonally saturated, with a midrange that is pure magic on vocals. It eschews clinical analysis for profound musical beauty and emotional connection. A unique variation of the very-high-compliance XLM cartridge, the Integra model came with the cartridge body integrated into a lightweight carbon fibre headshell. This was designed to optimize the mass and rigidity for the cartridge, ensuring it performed its best. It delivered the classic delicate, airy, and detailed XLM sound in a convenient package for standard tonearms. A classic and highly influential MC cartridge from Ortofon in the 1980s. The MC 20 Super was renowned for its neutral, refined, and beautifully balanced sound. It used a Fine Line stylus to provide excellent detail retrieval without any harshness. It was a benchmark for performance and musicality in its time and is still highly regarded. A mid-point in A-T's VM500 series, the 530EN features a nude elliptical stylus. This provides a clear step-up in detail, clarity, and high-frequency refinement compared to bonded elliptical styli. It's a lively and detailed performer that offers a great balance of performance and price. A legendary MC cartridge from Fidelity Research, a company renowned for its high-end tonearms. The FR-1 Mk3 was known for its powerful, dynamic, and authoritative sound, combined with excellent detail and tracking from its line-contact stylus. It's a classic example of the high-performance Japanese MC cartridges of the era. The entry-level model in the Excel ES-70 series. The 'S' (Spherical) model was a solid, reliable performer from this major Japanese OEM. It offered a warm, musical, and forgiving sound, making it a good, no-fuss choice for a wide variety of turntables and records. A classic high-performance MC from Sumiko in the 1980s. The Talisman S (Sapphire cantilever was also an option) was known for its speed, detail, and neutrality. It was a very agile and revealing cartridge that excelled at capturing transients and complex musical passages with precision. A true audiophile classic. The robust, conical-stylus version of the popular M75 series. The M75B was a workhorse cartridge, often found on automatic changers and consoles. It offered a warm, punchy, and forgiving sound that was great for rock and pop, and its robust design could handle less-than-ideal conditions. Miyajima's reference-level true mono cartridge. The Zero is designed to extract the maximum amount of musical information from mono LPs. It delivers a sound that is incredibly solid, powerful, and authoritative, with a sense of scale and presence that is breathtaking. It is considered by many to be one of the finest mono cartridges ever made. The Grado Prestige Silver is a specially selected version of the Green model, chosen for meeting even higher test specifications. It offers the classic warm, rich Grado sound with its famous forward midrange, making it highly musical and engaging, especially with vocals. A step up in quality control within the Prestige line. A sophisticated MC cartridge from Pro-Ject, made by Ortofon to their specifications. The Pick-IT DS2 features a polyamide body for resonance control and offers a smooth, detailed, and highly musical presentation. It's a significant step up from entry-level cartridges, providing a taste of high-end refinement. The SPU Classic A features the original 'A-type' shorter headshell, making it a perfect match for vintage tonearms designed for this geometry. It delivers the same weighty, dynamic, and tonally rich sound as the G-shell version, offering a presentation that is beloved for its scale, authority, and musical substance on classic recordings. A popular and highly-regarded MM cartridge from the 1980s. The AT120E was known for its bright, clear, and detailed sound. It offered excellent tracking and a lively presentation that brought a lot of energy to recordings. It was a step up from the AT95E, providing more refinement and high-frequency extension. Hana's flagship cartridge, the Umami Red combines key components from the ML series with an Auricle body (ebony wood with Urushi lacquer) and concepts from the classic Hana sound. It achieves a stunning synthesis of detail and musicality, offering a rich, powerful, and organic sound that is both highly resolving and deeply emotional. A true statement product. Often bundled with the higher-end versions of the Concept turntable, the Concept MC embodies the Clearaudio house sound. It is a highly precise, clean, and detailed cartridge that offers a neutral window into the recording. The MicroLine stylus and boron cantilever ensure excellent tracking and resolution. A top-tier MM cartridge from Empire in the late 1960s. The 1000ZE/X was known for its warm, smooth, and full-bodied sound. It tracked at incredibly low forces for its time and was praised for its musical, non-fatiguing presentation, competing directly with the top models from Shure. Linn's current flagship moving magnet cartridge. The Adikt is designed to deliver a highly musical and engaging performance, with the classic Linn focus on rhythm and timing. The Gyger II line-contact stylus ensures excellent detail retrieval, resulting in a sound that is both fun and insightful. Features Linn's three-point mounting system. A high-performance MM cartridge from UK-based Vertere Acoustics. The Sabre is designed for dynamic expression and precision. It features an aluminum body with a three-point contact system for optimal coupling to the headshell. The sound is punchy and detailed with excellent timing and precise stereo imaging. Audio Note's top-of-the-line moving magnet cartridge. Based on the Goldring 1000-series body, the IQ3 is heavily modified with a titanium cantilever, advanced stylus, and silver coils. It is renowned for its natural tonality, dynamic freedom, and deeply musical presentation, embodying the Audio Note philosophy of emotional engagement over clinical analysis. The Dynavector 10x5 MkII represents a refined iteration of a classic high-output moving coil cartridge.  Incorporating an upgraded body and Shibata stylus, it delivers a more nuanced and detailed sonic performance than its elliptical counterpart while retaining the characteristic Dynavector dynamic punch and rhythmic drive. This makes it a highly versatile and exceptional performer. A long-running and highly-regarded MM cartridge from UK manufacturer Roksan. Based on the Goldring 1000-series body, the Corus2 is selected and housed in a special metal body for improved resonance control. It's known for a dynamic, punchy, and detailed sound with an excellent sense of rhythm and timing. A popular high-output moving coil cartridge. The MC-1 Turbo provides a clean, clear, and dynamic sound that offers a taste of MC speed and resolution without the need for a dedicated MC phono stage. It's a solid and reliable performer that represents a good upgrade from entry-level MM cartridges. A vintage predecessor to the well-known M97xE. The M97HE (Hyperelliptical) was a top performer, positioned just below the V15 series. It featured a line-contact stylus for excellent tracking and detail retrieval. Its sound was famously neutral and smooth, embodying the classic, accurate Shure house sound. Often came with the Dynamic Stabilizer brush. A classic entry-level MM cartridge often bundled with Dual turntables in the 1970s. The DMS 200 was a reliable performer with a warm, musical, and forgiving sound. Its conical stylus was robust and kind to records, making it a solid, no-fuss choice for the turntables of the era. A step up from the Quintet Red, the Blue model features a Nude Elliptical stylus. This provides a more open, clear, and detailed sound with better resolution and dynamics. It's a very capable all-rounder that offers a more transparent window into the recording. A significant step up from the entry-level OC9X, the XEN features a nude elliptical stylus on a boron cantilever. This combination delivers a faster, clearer, and more detailed sound with improved transient response and high-frequency refinement. A superb mid-tier MC performer. A modern MC from Linn, the Kendo is positioned below the flagship Kandid. It features a nickel-coated 7075-grade aluminium body and a boron cantilever with a Vital stylus. It delivers a superb balance of detail retrieval and the classic Linn musicality and rhythmic drive (PRaT), making it an excellent match for high-end Linn systems. Rega's flagship moving coil cartridge, the Aphelion 2 is the result of years of R&D. It features a unique hand-polished fine line stylus on a boron cantilever with a proprietary 'zero-tolerance' generator design. The sound is the ultimate expression of Rega's philosophy: incredibly fast, transparent, and dynamically expressive, revealing the music with breathtaking clarity and timing. Soundsmith's flagship MI cartridge, the Hyperion is unique for its use of a hand-selected, treated cactus spine for the cantilever. This organic material is said to have excellent damping properties, resulting in a sound that is incredibly natural, detailed, and musically coherent. It's a true reference-level performer designed for ultimate musical enjoyment. A later version of the legendary 681EEE, the II-S featured a Stereohedron line-contact stylus for improved high-frequency tracking. It maintained the series' reputation as a professional calibration standard, delivering a famously neutral, accurate, and honest sound. The 'S' indicates it was intended for both stereo and matrix quadraphonic use. Pickering's top-of-the-line cartridge for CD-4 discrete quadraphonic systems. The XUV/4500Q featured a 'Quadrahedral' stylus (a Shibata variant) for its extended high-frequency response. It was a superb tracker and highly detailed performer, but required specific 100k Ohm loading to sound its best. A vintage high-tech marvel. A top-tier MM cartridge in Empire's highly regarded 2000 series. The 2000Z was praised for its balanced and clear presentation, with excellent detail and outstanding channel separation. It was a formidable competitor to the top models from Shure and Stanton, offering a slightly different but equally valid high-fidelity sound. The final and most advanced version of Technics' legendary integrated headshell cartridge. The EPC-100C Mk4 used a pure boron pipe cantilever and a nude Shibata stylus to achieve astonishingly wide frequency response and resolution. It's a true reference-level transducer, designed for ultimate accuracy and transparency. A technical tour de force. A high-end MC from Vertere, the Mystic is designed to deliver music with an effortless and natural flow. It combines a solid aluminum body with a boron cantilever and a micro-elliptical stylus. The sound is highly detailed and dynamic but is particularly praised for its superb sense of timing and musical coherence, making it incredibly engaging. A legendary MC cartridge from Clearaudio, famous for its hand-polished ebony wood body. The Stradivari V2 is praised for combining Clearaudio's signature detail and precision with a rich, natural, and warm tonality, reminiscent of the famous violins it's named after. It is a highly musical and emotionally satisfying high-end performer. A classic high-performance model from ZYX. The Ultimate 100 embodies the brand's 'Real Stereo' philosophy, aiming for perfect time and phase alignment. The result is a sound that is exceptionally fast, clean, and transparent, with outstanding rhythm and pace. It offers a clear, uncolored view of the music, with superb detail and dynamics. A step up from the Rosewood, the Koetsu Urushi models feature a Rosewood body that has been hand-lacquered in the traditional Urushi style. This process is said to enhance the body's resonance properties, resulting in an even more refined and sublime version of the classic Koetsu sound. It is a true work of audio art, both sonically and visually. A highly sought-after vintage variant of the classic DL-103. The DL-103S featured an elliptical stylus and a more compliant suspension. This resulted in a sound that was more detailed and refined than the standard conical version, while being suitable for a wider range of medium-mass tonearms. A classic for a reason. The low-output version of the classic Eroica. The LX (Low Output) requires a dedicated MC phono stage and is often considered to be slightly more refined and transparent than its high-output 'H' sibling. It offers a clean, agile, and open sound with excellent detail retrieval from its Gyger II stylus. A low-output Moving Iron cartridge from Grado's Timbre series. The Master3 features a Cocobolo wood body and a boron cantilever. It offers a more dynamic and detailed presentation than the Sonata3, while still retaining the gloriously rich and textured midrange that is the Grado hallmark. A very refined and engaging performer. The entry-level MM cartridge from Mobile Fidelity. The StudioTracker uses a bonded elliptical stylus but shares the V-Twin dual-magnet generator with its more expensive siblings. It offers a balanced and clear sound that is faithful to MoFi's 'original master recording' philosophy, representing great value and a solid starting point. The Sumiko Olympia offers a balanced sonic signature with smooth highs and a refined presentation.  It provides improved detail and texture compared to entry-level models. A key model in the OC9X series, the XSH features a Shibata stylus on a boron cantilever. Compared to the analytical MicroLine version, the Shibata offers a smoother, richer, and more tonally dense presentation. It excels at delivering high-frequency detail without any hint of stridency, making it a very refined and musical choice. The more hi-fi oriented sibling of the legendary M44-7. The M44G tracked at a much lower force and had a more balanced sound, with less pronounced bass. It was a favorite for radio stations and general purpose DJing, offering a great combination of durability, high output, and punchy, dynamic sound. A special edition cartridge released to celebrate the 250th anniversary of Ludwig van Beethoven's birth. The 2M Black LVB 250 uses the same stylus/cantilever as the MC Cadenza Black and a new, improved suspension rubber. This results in a sound that is more dynamic, open, and refined than the already excellent standard 2M Black. The low-output version of the entry-level Hana EH. The EL (Elliptical, Low-output) requires a dedicated MC phono stage and is often considered to have a slight edge in refinement and transparency over its high-output sibling. It is widely praised for its exceptional musicality and smooth, balanced presentation at a very competitive price. Positioned between the Ania and Apheta 3, the Ania Pro uses the advanced Vital stylus profile found on the more expensive models. This provides a significant increase in detail and refinement over the standard Ania, while maintaining the characteristic Rega energy and rhythmic drive. A superb performer. The successor to the Linn Troika, the Klyde was a long-running MC model for Linn. It offered a solid, weighty, and powerful presentation with a strong bass foundation. It was a very musical and coherent performer that continued the Linn tradition of making music engaging and enjoyable. A legendary reference cartridge, the Benz-Micro Ruby Z features a solid ruby cantilever. It is famous for its sweet, warm, and liquid presentation, combined with world-class detail retrieval and holographic imaging. It's a cartridge that prioritizes tonal beauty and emotional connection, representing the pinnacle of the classic 'Benz' sound. A high-end, high-output Moving Iron cartridge from Soundsmith. The Voice uses a ruby cantilever and an advanced line-contact stylus to achieve exceptional detail, dynamics, and transparency. It is particularly praised for its natural and realistic reproduction of vocals and instruments, truly living up to its name. The professional, high-output version of the reference Stanton 881. The 981 HZS (High-output, Calibrated, Stereohedron) was individually calibrated and intended for mastering studios and broadcast. It offered the same neutral, highly detailed sound as its low-output siblings but with a powerful output signal. A true reference tool. A very popular and affordable low-output MC from Audio-Technica. The AT-F7 offers a significant taste of moving coil performance—speed, clarity, and detail—at a price point usually dominated by MM cartridges. It's a clean, clear, and well-balanced performer that represents an excellent entry into the world of LOMC. EAT's reference moving coil cartridge. The Jo N°8 features a solid chestnut wood body, a Shibata stylus on a boron cantilever, and high-purity silver coils. It delivers a rich, dynamic, and highly detailed sound with a great sense of refinement and musicality. A true high-end performer that combines aesthetic beauty with top-tier sonics. A refined version of the classic SPU, the Meister Silver uses high-purity silver coils and a nude elliptical stylus. This provides a more open, detailed, and transparent sound compared to the standard SPU models, while still retaining the characteristic SPU weight, power, and dynamic authority. A great blend of classic and modern virtues. The flagship of Nagaoka's new Jeweltone (JT) series. The JT-80BK (Black) features a nude elliptical stylus on a boron cantilever. It offers a more modern, clear, and detailed sound compared to the classic warmth of the MP series. It's a powerful and dynamic performer with excellent clarity and resolution. Another sought-after vintage variant, the DL-103D was, like the 103S, a higher-compliance version of the standard DL-103. It featured a more advanced elliptical stylus, resulting in greater detail and refinement. It was designed to be compatible with a wider range of tonearms than the rugged, low-compliance broadcast original. The pinnacle of the Koetsu line. The Signature Platinum models use platinum magnets and silver-plated copper coils, representing the ultimate expression of Sugano-san's art. It combines the legendary Koetsu richness and magical midrange with a level of detail, refinement, and dynamic expression that is world-class. A truly reference-level cartridge for the discerning music lover. Positioned just below Lyra's flagship Atlas, the Etna is a true reference-level performer. It uses an asymmetric, resonance-controlled body and a diamond-coated boron cantilever. The sound is incredibly transparent, dynamic, and detailed, with a sense of realism and presence that is breathtaking. It combines neutrality with a powerful musical drive. The magnetic-core sibling to the non-magnetic ART9XA. The ART9XI is designed for a vibrant, dynamic, and detailed sound that captures the energy of a live performance. It offers a more forward and exciting presentation compared to the warmer XA, making it a thrilling and highly resolving cartridge. The entry-level model in Nagaoka's famed MP series. The MP-100 uses a conical stylus, providing a warm, forgiving, and fundamentally musical sound. While it lacks the detail of its more expensive siblings, it captures the essential warmth and quiet groove-riding ability that Nagaoka is known for. A classic Shure MM cartridge from the 1960s. The M55E was a versatile and popular choice, known for its warm, dynamic, and punchy sound. It was an excellent match for the rock and pop music of the era, delivering a fun and engaging performance. It was often paired with turntables from Dual and Garrard. The Ortofon MC Windfeld Ti is a high-end moving coil cartridge renowned for its accuracy and resolving power.  Its design, incorporating an SLM-manufactured titanium body and the Replicant 100 stylus, contributes to a remarkably neutral tonal balance. This cartridge delivers exceptional detail retrieval and a vast, transparent soundstage with impactful dynamics, setting a benchmark for accuracy and resolution. Linn's flagship moving coil cartridge. The Kandid features a nude, open-body design to eliminate housing resonance. It delivers an incredibly detailed and transparent sound while maintaining the crucial Linn qualities of rhythm and musical flow. It is designed to be the ultimate source component, revealing the maximum amount of music from the groove. Mobile Fidelity's flagship moving coil cartridge. The UltraGold MC features a Shibata stylus on a boron cantilever and is designed to be the ultimate transducer, adhering to the 'original master recording' philosophy. It is praised for its neutrality, transparency, and high resolution, delivering a sound that is exceptionally true to the source material. Benz-Micro's flagship cartridge, the LP-S (Low-output, S-class) is a true reference performer. It features an aged ebony body and a massive, low-vibration generator. The sound is authoritative and powerful, with a huge, enveloping soundstage, combined with the classic Benz warmth and rich tonality. It is a deeply musical and emotionally satisfying top-tier cartridge. The flagship of Grado's Lineage Series, the Epoch3 is a cost-no-object statement piece. It features a Cocobolo wood body, a sapphire cantilever, and a unique diamond stylus. It represents the ultimate realization of the Grado sound: effortlessly natural, with a sublime, lifelike midrange and an incredibly immersive and realistic presentation. A true end-game cartridge for lovers of musical realism. A popular choice from A-T's VM500 series, the 520EB (Elliptical Bonded) is a fantastic value proposition. It offers a well-balanced, clear, and punchy sound that is a significant upgrade from entry-level conical styli. A great all-round performer that tracks well and provides an engaging listen. A true mono MC cartridge within the Quintet series. It is internally wired to only respond to lateral groove movement, which significantly reduces surface noise on mono LPs. It delivers a solid, focused, and powerful mono image, providing a much more authentic and enjoyable experience for mono record collectors than a strapped-stereo cartridge. A very rare and sought-after variant of the DL-103. The 'M' model was a lightweight, high-compliance version featuring a boron cantilever and a very low output voltage. It was designed for low-mass tonearms and delivered a much lighter, faster, and more detailed sound compared to the standard 103, making it a very different beast altogether. An evolution ('EV') of the classic AT33 series. The 33EV features a nude elliptical stylus on a duralumin tapered cantilever. Compared to its line-contact siblings, it offers a warmer, smoother, and more forgiving sound with more body, while still providing excellent MC detail and speed. A very musical and non-fatiguing listen. The first generation of Rega's high-end Apheta MC. It established the series' reputation for a very fast, detailed, and dynamic sound. The one-piece aluminum body and unique generator design contributed to its revealing and energetic character. A true taste of high-end speed and resolution. The entry-level model in Nagaoka's new Jeweltone (JT) series. The JT-80LB (Lapis Blue) uses a bonded conical stylus. It delivers a fun, warm, and punchy sound with good bass presence, offering a modern take on the classic entry-level musical sound. A well-regarded budget MM cartridge known for its smooth, warm, and forgiving nature. It has a strong and weighty bass response and a pleasant midrange, making it very musical and non-fatiguing. It may lack the ultimate detail of some competitors but is a great choice for taming brighter systems. A fascinating and tiny MC cartridge from Sony's golden era. The XL-MC1 was incredibly small and light, featuring a beryllium cantilever for speed and detail. It was often sold with its own dedicated headshell (SH-165). The sound is fast, clear, and detailed, representing Sony's high-tech approach to audio in the early 80s. A classic high-end MC cartridge from British manufacturer Mission. The 773 featured a sapphire cantilever and was known for its dynamic and highly musical presentation. It offered excellent detail retrieval without sounding clinical, with a slightly warm character that made it very engaging. A rare and sought-after vintage piece. A classic MM cartridge from the company that would become Arcam. The C77 was a very popular and well-regarded performer in the UK market. It offered a smooth, warm, and highly musical sound that was a great match for turntables like the Rega Planar 3. A fine example of the classic 'British sound'. A solid mid-range MM cartridge from Pioneer's hi-fi heyday. The PC-330 was a capable and well-balanced performer, often bundled with Pioneer's own PL-series turntables. It delivered a clear, detailed, and enjoyable sound that was competitive with other Japanese cartridges of the era. A legendary and very rare MC cartridge from Japanese manufacturer Coral. The 777-EX was a high-end statement piece, featuring a Shibata stylus on a beryllium cantilever. It was known for its exceptional resolution, speed, and transparency, competing with the very best cartridges of its time. A true collector's item. Glanz was a respected Japanese manufacturer of tonearms and cartridges. The MFG-310LX was a well-made MM cartridge featuring a carbon fibre cantilever, which was an advanced material for its time. It delivered a smooth, musical, and nicely textured sound, with a character that was both enjoyable and sophisticated. A statement-level MC cartridge from Japanese high-end audio manufacturer Air Tight. The PC-1 Supreme is renowned for its incredibly powerful dynamics, transparency, and resolving power. It uses high-performance magnets and an advanced generator design to create a sound that is both authoritative and supremely detailed, placing it in the top tier of reference cartridges. Renowned for their ultra-low impedance designs, My Sonic Lab cartridges, such as the Eminent EX, consistently impress with their ability to deliver a quiet background from which intricate details blossom with clarity. The result is a highly refined and transparent sound reproduction, remaining neutral and free of unwanted colorations. A now-discontinued but highly-regarded MC cartridge. Transfiguration was known for its 'yokeless' ring-magnet design, which aimed for superior phase coherence. The Axia S was praised for its natural, coherent, and deeply musical presentation. It offered plenty of detail but in a very organic and non-fatiguing way, drawing the listener into the performance. The workhorse of the Ortofon DJ Concorde line. The Pro S is a no-nonsense, robust, and reliable cartridge designed for general club and mixing use. It offers a balanced sound, good tracking ability, and is kinder to records than more specialized scratch cartridges. A solid, all-round DJ tool. The low-output version of the Songbird. The 'IO' (Input/Output, for low output) requires a dedicated MC phono stage and is generally considered to offer a slightly higher level of transparency and refinement than its high-output sibling. The use of a boron cantilever provides a clear, fast, and transparent sound with excellent detail. A special edition of the DL-103 released in the 1980s. The DL-103C1 featured high-purity LC-OFC (Linear Crystal Oxygen-Free Copper) coils. This resulted in a more transparent and refined sound compared to the standard model, with better resolution of low-level detail, while retaining the powerful dynamic drive of the original. The top-of-the-line version of the M95 series, the M95HE featured a nude hyperelliptical stylus. This brought its performance very close to the more expensive V15 series, earning it the nickname 'a baby V15'. It offered a highly detailed, smooth, and neutral sound with excellent tracking ability. A superb vintage performer. The Grado Prestige Red is a specially selected version of the Blue model, chosen for meeting higher test specifications. It offers the classic warm, rich Grado sound with its famous forward midrange, making it highly musical and engaging, especially with vocals. A step up in quality control within the Prestige line. A legendary and now-discontinued MM cartridge, the AT150MLX was famous for its incredible detail retrieval. The MicroLine stylus on a gold-plated boron cantilever provided superb tracking and high-frequency extension. Its sound was often described as clean and detailed, but could lean towards brightness, making it a revealing but sometimes demanding cartridge. A benchmark for MM resolution. A modern take on the SPU, the Synergy is a high-output model designed to be compatible with standard MM phono stages. It uses neodymium magnets and silver-plated copper wire to achieve its higher output. It delivers a rich, dynamic, and powerful sound, making the classic SPU experience accessible to a wider range of systems. A top-tier P-Mount (T4P) cartridge from ADC. The PSX-40 was an induced magnet design that offered excellent performance for the P-Mount standard. It was known for a clear, detailed, and well-balanced sound, making it a significant upgrade for many linear tracking and other T4P-equipped turntables. The flagship Lyra cartridge, the Atlas is a true cost-no-object design. The SL (Single Layer) version has lower output and is considered by some to be even more transparent. It uses an asymmetrical titanium body and a unique generator design to achieve state-of-the-art performance. The sound is the epitome of transparency, resolution, and dynamics, setting a benchmark for what is possible in vinyl playback. The Grado Prestige Blue is a specially selected version of the Black model, chosen for meeting higher test specifications. It offers the classic warm, rich Grado sound with its famous forward midrange, making it highly musical and engaging, especially with vocals. A step up in quality control within the Prestige line. The entry-level model in Audio Note's MM cartridge range. Based on the Goldring 1000-series body, the IQ1 offers a highly musical and dynamic performance. It captures the essence of the Audio Note sound, prioritizing emotional engagement and tonal richness over sterile detail. A great introduction to the brand. A limited-edition cartridge released for Ortofon's 100th anniversary. The SPU Century is a statement piece, featuring a wood and SLM-titanium body, silver coils, and a nude Shibata stylus. It represents the pinnacle of the SPU concept, blending the legendary power and richness with a high degree of detail, refinement, and transparency. A collector's item and a sonic masterpiece. The flagship of the VM700 series, the 760SLC features A-T's most advanced Special Line Contact stylus. This provides the ultimate in detail retrieval, transparency, and channel separation for the MM line. It's a true high-resolution transducer that aims for maximum accuracy and clarity, competing with high-end MCs in its resolving power. A classic entry-level MM cartridge from Philips, often found on their turntables from the 1970s. The GP 400 was a solid, reliable performer with a warm, pleasant, and musical sound. Its conical stylus was robust and forgiving of record imperfections, making it a popular choice for everyday listening. A versatile cartridge designed as a hybrid for both hi-fi listening and DJ use. The M35X combined the ruggedness and high output needed for club use with an elliptical stylus that provided better detail and frequency response than a standard conical.
