It was known for a punchy, dynamic, and engaging sound. A high-quality cartridge from Japanese OEM Excel Sound, designed for CD-4 discrete quadraphonic playback. The QD-700C featured a Shibata stylus for its extended high-frequency capabilities. It was a smooth, detailed, and refined performer that required the specific 100k Ohm loading typical of CD-4 cartridges. A classic model in the legendary Karat series. The 23R featured a short (2.3mm) solid ruby cantilever, which provided exceptional stiffness and low mass. This resulted in the characteristic Karat sound: incredibly fast, dynamic, and detailed, with razor-sharp transients and precise imaging. A vintage high-performance icon. A high-performance MC from Sumiko that was a precursor to the famous Talisman series. The Premier MMT was built by Supex and was known for its fast, detailed, and refined sound. It was an excellent tracker and offered a taste of high-end performance with great clarity and musical insight. The entry point to the discontinued Rondo series. The Rondo Red featured a body made from a wood/resin composite. It was known for its warm, dynamic, and musically engaging sound. A great all-round performer that was less analytical and more focused on musical enjoyment. The middle model in Audio Note's MM cartridge range. The IQ2 upgrades the IQ1 with a more advanced 'Type 1' line-contact stylus and a titanium cantilever. This results in improved detail retrieval and refinement, while maintaining the core Audio Note qualities of natural tonality and dynamic, musical engagement. The entry-level moving coil from turntable manufacturer Michell Engineering. The Cusis E features a large composite body to control resonances. It offers a smooth, balanced, and highly musical presentation, making it a great match for Michell's own turntables and a solid entry into the world of MC cartridges. A high-output MC cartridge from Italian manufacturer Gold Note. The Donatello Red features a duralumin body and is known for its dynamic, vibrant, and highly musical sound. It captures the life and energy in the music, offering an engaging and passionate presentation. Compatible with standard MM phono stages. A step up from the MC-1 Turbo, the MC-3 Turbo featured a more advanced Fine Line stylus. This provided a more detailed and refined sound with improved tracking of high frequencies. It was a very popular high-output MC, offering a taste of high-end resolution without the need for an MC phono stage. A popular P-Mount (T4P) cartridge from Stanton. The L720EE was part of their 'Linear-contact' series and provided a clear, well-balanced sound with good tracking ability. It was a reliable and solid performer for the many turntables that used the T4P standard. A modern workhorse in the Concorde line, the Mix is designed as a great all-rounder for DJs. It offers a good balance of output, sound quality, and tracking ability, making it suitable for mixing, beatmatching, and light scratching. A solid, reliable tool for any DJ. The high-output version of the acclaimed DV-20X2. The 'H' model offers the same refined and detailed sound, with a powerful and controlled bass response, but its higher output allows it to be used with standard MM phono stages. A very sophisticated and versatile performer. The predecessor to the legendary MP-110. The Nagaoka MP-11 was a hugely popular cartridge, establishing the brand's reputation for warm, musical, and quiet-running cartridges. The conical stylus version offered a forgiving and highly enjoyable sound, making it a favorite for many music lovers. The entry point to Sumiko's modern Oyster series. The Rainier offers a warm, smooth, and musical sound with a pleasant and solid bass foundation. It's a great all-rounder that is easy to listen to and provides a clear upgrade path within the series (stylus is interchangeable with Olympia and Moonstone). A very popular entry-level cartridge, often found as standard equipment on turntables from Pro-Ject and other manufacturers. The OM 5E offers a clear, balanced, and lively sound that is a significant step up from basic conical styli. Its lightweight body makes it a good match for low-mass tonearms. The second iteration of the legendary V15 'Super-Track' series. The Type II improved upon the original with even better trackability and a smoother, more neutral frequency response. It was a benchmark for accuracy in its time and established the V15 as the go-to cartridge for serious audiophiles and record reviewers. A well-made MM cartridge from Sony. The XL-33 featured a carbon fibre cantilever for good stiffness and low mass, resulting in a clean, detailed, and precise sound. It was a solid performer in the competitive Japanese market of the late 70s and early 80s. A budget-friendly MM cartridge from Goldring's classic era. The G850 was a robust and reliable performer with a warm and punchy sound. It was a popular choice for entry-level to mid-range turntables and was known for being a forgiving and musically enjoyable listen. A legendary vintage MM cartridge, the predecessor to the AT150MLX. The AT-ML150 featured a beryllium cantilever and one of the first MicroLine styli, making it a technical marvel. It offered state-of-the-art detail retrieval and tracking, with a characteristically clean, bright, and highly resolving sound. A benchmark for its time. A step up in the classic OM (Optimum Match) series, the OM 20 features a nude elliptical stylus. This provides a more refined, detailed, and clear sound compared to the bonded stylus on the OM 10. It's a neutral and highly capable performer, well-suited to a wide range of music and low-to-medium mass tonearms. A high-end model in the classic OM series, the OM 30 features a nude Fine Line stylus. This offers superb tracking and detail retrieval, approaching the performance of more expensive MC cartridges. The sound is highly detailed, neutral, and refined. A top-tier vintage MM performer for low-mass tonearms. A very common entry-level MM cartridge from Pioneer, often found on their turntables from the 70s and 80s. The PC-110 was a solid, reliable performer with a warm and musically forgiving sound. A good basic cartridge that brought music to life for many new hi-fi owners. The original Blue Point was a landmark high-output MC cartridge that made the moving coil sound accessible to a wide audience. It was known for its bold, dynamic, and slightly forward presentation that was exciting and engaging. A true classic that paved the way for its famous successor, the Blue Point No. 2. A special 'Gold Limited' edition of the DL-103. The DL-103GL featured high-purity gold wire for the coils, which was said to provide a smoother and more refined sound than both the standard copper and the LC-OFC wire of other special editions. A rare and luxurious take on a broadcast classic. A legendary reference-level MC cartridge. The Audio Note IO I has an extremely low internal impedance and output voltage, requiring a dedicated step-up transformer (SUT). It is famous for its incredibly lifelike, dynamic, and natural presentation. It's a cartridge that communicates the emotion and soul of the music with unparalleled realism. The flagship of the classic OM series. The OM 40 features Ortofon's top-tier Fritz Gyger 70 line-contact stylus, providing state-of-the-art tracking and resolution for an MM cartridge. Its sound is hyper-detailed, transparent, and neutral, sometimes leaning towards analytical. A true reference-level performer for low-mass tonearms. A classic all-round DJ cartridge from the Concorde series. The DJ S model was designed to be a solid performer for all styles of DJing, from mixing to light scratching. It offers a punchy sound, good tracking stability, and the durability needed for professional use. A very popular model for many years. A modern high-output, true mono cartridge designed for mono LPs. It's internally wired to only read the lateral signal, which greatly reduces noise on mono records. It offers a solid, powerful, and focused sound image, making it an excellent choice for listeners wanting to properly experience their mono collection without needing a dedicated MC phono stage. Developed in collaboration with legendary turntablist DJ Q.Bert, this cartridge is engineered specifically for scratching and back-cueing. It has a ridiculously high output of 11mV for a massive, punchy sound and a specially developed suspension for unmatched groove-holding ability. The ultimate tool for scratch DJs. The low-output version of the Donatello, the Gold model requires a dedicated MC phono stage. It offers a more refined and detailed sound than its high-output Red sibling, with improved transparency and nuance. It retains the vibrant, dynamic, and musically engaging character that Gold Note is known for. A step up from the Kiseki Blue, the Purple Heart N.S. features a solid Purpleheart wood body and a more advanced line-contact stylus. This results in a more detailed and powerful presentation, with enhanced dynamics and resolution, while still retaining the rich, colourful, and deeply musical character that defines the Kiseki brand. Made for Pro-Ject by Ortofon to celebrate the 25th anniversary of the Pro-Ject 1 turntable. The Pick-IT 25A is essentially a variant of the Ortofon 2M series (close to a 2M Silver) with silver-plated copper coils. It offers a lively, clear, and detailed sound that is a significant step up from entry-level cartridges. The high-output version of the flagship M-Series. The MH (Microline, High-output) offers the same exceptional resolution and transparency as the ML, but its 2.0mV output allows it to be used with standard MM phono stages. It is praised for its superb stereo imaging and quiet backgrounds, bringing reference-level performance to a wider range of systems. The elliptical stylus version of the classic G800. The 'E' model was a very popular upgrade, offering improved detail retrieval and high-frequency extension compared to the standard conical stylus, while retaining the warm, powerful, and musical character of the G800 family. A great vintage all-rounder. A classic high-performance MC from Sumiko in the 1980s. The Talisman B (Boron cantilever) was known for its speed, detail, and neutrality. It was a very agile and revealing cartridge that excelled at capturing transients and complex musical passages with precision. A true audiophile classic, often seen as an alternative to the Sapphire cantilever version. A popular mid-range MM cartridge from Audio-Technica's vintage lineup. The AT13Ea was known for its clear, detailed, and slightly bright presentation. It was a step up from the AT11/12 series, offering greater refinement and better tracking. A very capable and well-regarded performer. A reference-level induced magnet cartridge from ADC, part of their final high-end series. The TRX-2 featured a nude line-contact stylus on a solid sapphire cantilever, an extremely advanced design for its time. It delivered a sound of exceptional detail, speed, and airiness, competing directly with the best from Shure and Stanton. Requires a very low-mass tonearm. A high-quality cartridge from B&O's Moving Micro Cross (MMC) series. The MMC 2 featured a nude elliptical diamond on a sapphire cantilever, offering very low effective tip mass. The sound is clear, refined, and detailed, with the smoothness characteristic of B&O's designs. Designed for their own very-low-mass tonearms. A reference-level MC cartridge from Clearaudio, featuring an aluminum body with a ceramic coating for resonance control. The Da Vinci V2 uses the Micro HD stylus and boron cantilever to deliver a sound of immense detail, dynamics, and neutrality. It is a highly accurate and revealing transducer, embodying the Clearaudio house sound at a very high level. A high-performance DJ cartridge designed for the most demanding applications. The 890 SA (Spherical/Elliptical?) was known for its superb tracking ability, making it a favorite for turntablists and scratch DJs. It offered a more detailed sound than many purely conical DJ carts, while still providing the ruggedness and high output required in a professional environment. A solid mid-range MM cartridge from Kenwood (Trio-Kenwood). The V-39 MkII was a capable all-rounder, often found on Kenwood's direct-drive turntables. It delivered a clear, balanced, and musically satisfying performance that was representative of the quality available from major Japanese manufacturers in that era. A specialized high-output, true mono cartridge designed specifically for playing 78 RPM shellac records. It features a larger 2.5 mil conical stylus that is correctly sized for the wider grooves of 78s. It's internally wired for mono to reduce noise and provides a clear, robust sound, making it an essential tool for any collector of 78 RPM records. The top elliptical model in Sumiko's modern Oyster series. The Moonstone features an upgraded generator and a smaller elliptical stylus compared to the Rainier and Olympia. This results in a more balanced, detailed, and clear presentation with improved channel separation and a wider soundstage. A very sophisticated MM performer. A step up from the basic DMS 200, the DMS 210 was a popular MM cartridge often bundled with Dual turntables. It featured an elliptical stylus, which provided better detail and high-frequency performance than its conical sibling, while still retaining a warm and musical character. The Shure M91G, a conical stylus version of the popular M91 series, is a high-compliance cartridge known for its warm, full and musical reproduction. Its forgiving nature and robust bass response made it a versatile performer for tonearms of its era. A rare version of the DL-103, the 'U' model was identical to the standard DL-103 but was supplied mounted in a universal headshell. This was a convenience for users who wanted a pre-mounted, ready-to-go solution. The sound is the classic, dynamic, and powerful presentation of the broadcast legend. The Ortofon MC Anna Diamond is a moving coil cartridge featuring a solid diamond cantilever and stylus, and an SLM-titanium body.  Its design prioritizes high-fidelity audio reproduction. The entry-level model in A-T's VM500 series. The 510CB features a bonded conical stylus, providing a warm, musical, and forgiving sound. It's a robust and reliable performer, and its compatibility with the other 500-series styli offers an excellent and easy upgrade path. The predecessor to the legendary F-9, the Grace F-8 series was also a highly-regarded line of MM cartridges. Known for a smooth, refined, and effortlessly musical presentation, the F-8 offered superb detail retrieval without ever sounding harsh. A true classic of the golden age of hi-fi, often paired with Grace's own tonearms. A very popular and versatile MM cartridge from the 70s. The M75EJ Type 2 was known for its warm, punchy, and engaging sound that was a great match for the rock and pop music of the era. A solid, reliable performer with a slightly heavier tracking force than the ED version, making it suitable for a wider range of tonearms. A high-end low-output MC cartridge from Gold Note. The Machiavelli Gold MkII features a line-contact stylus on a boron cantilever. It offers a highly dynamic, detailed, and refined sound, with the smooth and musical character that defines the brand's sonic signature. A top-tier performer. The P-Mount (T4P) version of the classic Ortofon OM 10. The OMP 10 was designed for linear tracking and other T4P-equipped turntables. It offered the same neutral, balanced, and detailed reproduction as its standard-mount sibling, making it a high-quality choice for this format. A high-quality MM cartridge from Empire's classic era. The 999VE/X was a high-compliance design known for its warm, smooth, and full-bodied sound. It was an excellent tracker at low forces and offered a highly musical and non-fatiguing presentation, competing with the best from Shure and Stanton. A popular and well-regarded MC cartridge from Yamaha. The MC-9 featured a beryllium cantilever for excellent speed and detail retrieval. Its sound was known for being neutral, clear, and refined, aligning with Yamaha's 'Natural Sound' philosophy. A very competent vintage MC. Roksan's reference moving coil cartridge. The Shiraz is a long-standing design, known for its powerful, dynamic, and weighty sound. It uses a generator from an EMT cartridge, housed in a solid aluminum body. The result is an authoritative and highly engaging cartridge that delivers music with scale and conviction. The flagship of Sumiko's modern Oyster MM series. The Amethyst uses a Nude Line-Contact stylus for excellent detail retrieval and high-frequency extension. It offers a very clean, balanced, and sophisticated sound that aims for accuracy and refinement. A classic entry-level MM cartridge from Audio-Technica's vintage lineup. The AT10 was a solid performer with a warm, musical, and forgiving sound. Its conical stylus was robust and kind to records, making it a reliable choice for many turntables of its era. A popular quadraphonic cartridge from Audio-Technica. The AT12S featured a Shibata stylus to handle the high-frequency carrier signal of CD-4 records. It was a smooth and detailed performer that required the 100k Ohm loading specific to the CD-4 system. A great example of the technology from the quadraphonic era. A very basic and robust entry-level cartridge from Shure. The M70B was designed for durability and reliability, often found in record players and consoles where ruggedness was key. It provides a warm and forgiving sound, suitable for playing records of all conditions. A significant step up in the discontinued Rondo series, the Bronze model featured Ortofon's excellent Fritz Gyger 70 line-contact stylus. This resulted in a sound that was much more detailed, neutral, and transparent than the Red and Blue models, offering a high level of refinement and resolution. Part of Ortofon's discontinued Rondo series, the MC Rondo Blue utilizes a nude Fine Line stylus, resulting in enhanced clarity and detail retrieval compared to its predecessor.  It offers a high-fidelity listening experience notable for its balance and refinement, representing an excellent value proposition in its class. The predecessor to the modern MP-150. The MP-15 offered a more balanced and refined sound than the MP-11. It retained the powerful bass Nagaoka is known for but integrated it better with a smoother midrange and more extended treble. A great all-rounder that combined musicality with more detail. A reference-level MM cartridge from Sony's golden era. The XL-55 featured a beryllium cantilever and a nude elliptical stylus for exceptional performance. It was an integrated headshell design, aiming for optimal rigidity and resonance control. The sound is highly detailed, fast, and airy, representing the pinnacle of Sony's MM technology. A true mono cartridge within the 2M series, specifically designed for playing 78 RPM shellac records. It features a larger 3.0 mil spherical stylus that is correctly sized for the wider grooves of 78s. It is internally strapped for mono to reduce noise and provides a clear, robust sound, essential for any collector of 78 RPM records. The successor to the popular Moonstone, the Wellfleet features a nude elliptical stylus and an upgraded generator housed in a new, more rigid body. This results in a highly balanced, detailed, and clear presentation with improved dynamics and channel separation. A very sophisticated MM performer. The quadraphonic version of the ES-70 series from Japanese OEM Excel Sound. The 'EX4' featured a Shibata stylus to handle the high-frequency carrier signal of CD-4 records. It was a smooth, detailed, and refined performer that required the specific 100k Ohm loading typical of CD-4 cartridges. A highly sought-after special edition of the DL-103 from the 1990s. The DL-103SL featured high-purity 6N silver wire for the coils. This resulted in a more transparent, airy, and refined sound compared to the standard model, with improved resolution of fine detail. A favorite among DL-103 connoisseurs. A high-quality MM cartridge from Acutex, a brand known for its tri-pole induced magnet design. The M312 STR (Symmetrical Tri-Radial) featured a line-contact stylus and was praised for its fast, clear, and detailed sound with excellent tracking ability. A respected vintage performer. Satin was a Japanese manufacturer famous for creating high-output moving coil cartridges with user-replaceable styli, a very rare feature. The M-117E was a popular model, known for its smooth, detailed, and musical sound. The beryllium cantilever provided excellent transient response, making it a highly capable and convenient MC. The ADC Astrion, a high-compliance moving magnet cartridge, was known for its exceptional detail retrieval and dynamic capabilities.  Its nude line-contact stylus and sapphire cantilever contributed to its reputation as a reference standard for its time.  Optimal performance is achieved with a low-mass tonearm. A reference cartridge from Ortofon's Exclusives series, the Verismo is named after the Italian opera style focused on realism. It features a solid diamond cantilever and Replicant 100 stylus in an SLM-titanium body. The sound is exceptionally transparent, dynamic, and tonally natural, aiming to create a sense of 'being there' with lifelike realism. A solid MM cartridge from Marantz, likely manufactured for them by a major Japanese OEM like Excel Sound. The CT-5 was often bundled with Marantz turntables in the 70s and 80s. It provided a warm, smooth, and musical sound that was characteristic of the brand's sonic signature and easy to enjoy. A high-output moving coil cartridge from Scientific Audio Electronics (SAE). The 1000E was known for its clear, detailed, and fast presentation, offering a taste of MC performance to a wider audience. It was a well-regarded cartridge from a company more famous for its powerful amplifiers and preamplifiers. The successor to the Linn Asak, the Asaka offered a more refined and controlled sound while maintaining the powerful rhythmic drive of its predecessor. It was a very popular choice for Linn LP12 owners in the early 1980s, delivering an exciting and musically engaging performance that was pure Linn. A specialized DJ cartridge designed for use with Digital Vinyl Systems (DVS) and timecode records. The Concorde Digital has a very high output (10mV) to provide a strong, clear signal to the DVS software, minimizing tracking errors. It also helps to reduce wear on timecode vinyl while still sounding good with regular records. The 'Single Layer' version of the acclaimed Kleos. This low-output variant is considered by many to be even more transparent and resolving, offering a stunningly clear and dynamic window into the recording. Requires a high-quality, high-gain phono stage. A legendary flagship cartridge featuring a body carved from solid onyx stone and a platinum magnet system. The Onyx Platinum is revered for its ability to combine state-of-the-art resolution with the sublime, organic texture and magical midrange that defines the Koetsu sound. A true audio jewel. A high-end MC featuring a ruby square plate generator, inspired by the flagship LP-S. The Gullwing SLR is known for its open, dynamic, and musically sweet presentation. It combines excellent detail retrieval with the classic warm and engaging Benz-Micro house sound. A legendary minimalist design from A.J. van den Hul. The Colibri has an extremely small body to reduce resonance and is known for its incredible speed, dynamics, and realism. The XGP (Grand Prix) version is a high-performance variant. It's a cartridge that is ruthlessly revealing but breathtakingly immediate. Positioned between the Umami Red and the ML, the Umami Blue uses a blue Auricle body and combines elements from both. It aims to deliver the rich musicality of the Red with the high resolution of the ML, resulting in a superbly balanced and engaging high-end cartridge. The high-trackability version of the popular M75. The M75ED Type 2 was a high-compliance design for low-mass arms, known for its warm, punchy, and engaging sound. An excellent all-rounder that was a step-up from the conical/heavier tracking versions. The MkII version of the legendary Stanton 881S. It continued the tradition of being a reference-level MI cartridge with a nude Stereohedron line-contact stylus for exceptional tracking and a neutral, clean, and highly refined sound. A direct competitor to the Shure V15 series. A step up from the XSV/3000, the 4000 model was another top-tier MI cartridge from Pickering. It offered a highly refined, smooth, and detailed sound, with the characteristic Pickering warmth and musicality. Featured the 'Dustamatic' brush and a nude Stereohedron stylus. A high-end, low-output Moving Iron cartridge from Soundsmith. The Sussurro is known for its exceptionally low noise floor and incredible detail retrieval and dynamics, thanks to its ruby cantilever and advanced stylus. It provides a highly resolved and transparent window into the recording. A true mono version of the classic SPU, designed for playing mono microgroove records. The 'G' shell is the standard long-body SPU. It offers a weighty, dynamic, and tonally rich sound that is beloved for its presentation of classical music and jazz. Not for stereo records. The stock tonearm on the high-end Kenwood KP-990 direct-drive turntable. It's a high-quality, medium-mass S-shaped arm with electronic lift, representative of the best of 1980s Japanese turntable engineering. A popular budget-friendly cartridge from ADC. The K8 was a robust performer with a conical stylus, known for its warm, musical, and forgiving sound. It was a solid choice for entry-level to mid-range turntables. A higher-end model from Supex, the SDX-1000 featured a boron cantilever and line-contact stylus. It offered a more refined and detailed sound than the classic SD-900, while retaining the brand's signature musicality and warmth. A highly sought-after vintage MC. A step up from the 501, the Shelter 901 III offers a more neutral and detailed presentation. It retains the powerful and engaging musicality of the Shelter house sound but with greater transparency and resolution. A top-tier performer. The Goldmund Reference 2 is known for its exceptional detail retrieval, dynamic range, and accurate imaging. It's often described as revealing and analytical, capable of uncovering subtleties in recordings that other cartridges might miss. However, its transparent nature may reveal flaws in a recording more prominently.                                                                                                                                                                                                       Grado's Statement /1 is considered a high-end moving iron cartridge renowned for its exceptional detail retrieval and dynamic range.  Reviewers often praise its transparency and ability to reproduce the nuances of recorded music.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  The Sansui SC-80 offers a pleasing listening experience with a forgiving nature and good musicality, making it suitable for a variety of musical genres.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           The Ortofon Cadenza Blue is a high-end moving coil cartridge praised for its exceptional detail retrieval, dynamic range, and natural tonal balance.  It offers a refined and engaging listening experience, making it suitable for audiophiles seeking high-fidelity reproduction. The Ortofon Cadenza Blue is a high-end moving coil cartridge renowned for its exceptional detail retrieval and dynamic range. Reviews consistently praise its musicality and ability to reveal subtle nuances in recordings. While demanding of a high-quality phono stage, it rewards the listener with an immersive and highly engaging listening experience.  It's known for its accurate imaging and precise transient response. Generally well-received for its musicality and dynamic range.  Reviewers consistently praise its ability to resolve fine details while maintaining a coherent and cohesive sound.  Some users find the cartridge to be particularly effective with revealing recordings.

The cartridges is manufactured using a special dampening material very similar to the one used for the high class Goldenote MC cartridges in order to achieve superior performance at a very convenient price.

The relatively simple body structure does not limit the use of the special fixing system allows easy and secure headshell fixing for higher quality selected arms installation.    The Baldinotti body is made from a solid piece of aluminium in order to eliminate all external unwanted vibrations.

Its output level is relatively low (0.3mV) but can be driven by most MC phono preamplifiers. The cantilever of the Baldinotti is conical and made of two pieces to avoid vibration. Its lightness and stiffness offer superior tracking ability.

The coils are hand wired and made with the best Oxygen Free Copper available. An internal dampening shirt made of the polymeric material called Sustarin is able to control most of unwanted external vibrations.

Despite as the other Baldinotti, the cantilever of the Aurum is made of two telescopic conical pieces for maximum performance. Its Oxygen Free Copper coils are lighter and even more precisely made than the others of the same series.    The Boboli is a high output MC cartridge with the body machined from a single piece of aluminium.

At its price level the Boboli is able to reproduce an outstanding powerful bass and an impressive degree of detail and dynamic.

The Boboli is compatible with many tone arms/turntable systems.   *3.54 cm/sec The Boboli Signature is a high output MC cartridge with the body machined from a single piece of aluminium and an internal dampening shirt made of a special polymeric material called Sustarin.

Its 1.8mV output level, is enough to be driven by most MM Phono Preamplifiers. The Stylus of the Boboli Signature is made of special Magnesium alloy to avoid vibrations; its lightness provides very good tracking ability. The coils are hand wired and they use the best Oxygen Free Copper available on the market.
